title: Towards Federated Learning on Time-Evolving Heterogeneous Data

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - TOWARDS FEDERATED LEARNING ON TIME EVOLVING HETEROGENEOUS DATA

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Federated Learning (FL) is an emerging learning paradigm that preserves privacy by ensuring client data locality on edge devices. The optimization of FL is challenging in practice due to the diversity and heterogeneity of the learning system. Despite recent research efforts on improving the optimization of heterogeneous data, the impact of time-varying heterogeneous data in real-world scenarios, such as changing client data or intermittent clients joining or leaving during training, has not been well studied. In this work, we propose Continual Federated Learning (CFL), a flexible framework, to capture the time-varying heterogeneity of FL. CFL covers complex and realistic scenarios—which are challenging to evaluate in previous FL formulations—by extracting the information of past local datasets and approximating the local objective functions. Theoretically, we demonstrate that CFL methods achieve a faster convergence rate than FedAvg in time-varying scenarios, with the benefit being dependent on approximation quality. In a series of experiments, we show that the numerical findings match the convergence analysis, and CFL methods significantly outperform the other SOTA FL baselines.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Federated Learning (FL) has recently emerged as a critical distributed machine learning paradigm to preserve user/client privacy. Clients engaged in the training process of FL only communicate their local model parameters, rather than their private local data, with the central server. As the workhorse algorithm in FL, FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017) performs multiple local stochastic gradient descent (SGD) updates on the available clients before communicating with the server. Despite its success, FedAvg suffers from the large heterogeneity (non-iid-ness) in the data presented on the different clients, causing drift in each client’s updates and resulting in slow and unstable convergence (Karimireddy et al., 2020b). To address this issue, a new line of study has been suggested lately that either simulates the distribution of the whole dataset using preassigned weights of clients (Wang et al., 2020; Reisizadeh et al., 2020; Mohri et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020a) or adopts variance reduction methods (Karimireddy et al., 2020b;a; Das et al., 2020; Haddadpour et al., 2021). However, the FL formulation in these approaches always assumes a fixed data distribution among clients throughout all training rounds, while in practice this assumption does not always hold because of the complex, uncontrolled, and unpredictable client behaviors. Local datasets, for example, often vary over time, and intermittent clients can join or depart during training without prior notice. The difficulty in addressing the time-varying heterogeneity of FL lies in the stateless nature of the local datasets, which includes the unpredictable future datasets and the impossibility of retaining all prior local datasets. To this end, we first provide a novel Continual Federated Learning (CFL) formulation and then propose a unified CFL framework as our solution. This framework encompasses a variety of design choices for approximating the local objective functions in the previous training rounds, with the difference between actual and estimated local object functions described as _information loss_ for further analysis.

To capture the time-varying data heterogeneity in the CFL framework, we expand the theoretical assumption on the client drift—which has been extensively utilized recently in Karimireddy et al. (2020b); Khaled et al. (2020); Li et al. (2020b)—to include both client drift and _time drift_ . This allows us to quantify the difference between local client objective function and global objective function for the considered time-varying heterogeneous clients. We provide convergence rates for our unified CFL framework in conjunction with information loss and new models of both client and time drifts. Our analysis reveals a faster convergence of CFL framework than FedAvg, with the benefit dependent on approximation quality. The rate of FedAvg obtained from

our framework is consistent with previous work, while a similarly simplified rate on Continual Learning (CL) (French, 1999; Kirkpatrick et al., 2017) for the convex and strongly convex cases is novel. Finally, in extensive empirical results, we demonstrate that CFL methods—stemmed from the CFL framework with different approximation techniques—significantly outperform the SOTA FL competitors on various realistic datasets. These numerical observations corroborate our theoretical findings.

**Contribution.** We summarize our key contributions below.

- We present a unified framework, termed Continual Federated Learning (CFL), together with a novel client and time drift modeling approach, to capture complex FL scenarios involving timevarying heterogeneous data. This is the first theoretical study, to our knowledge, that describes the time-varying nature of FL.

- We provide rigorous convergence analysis for the CFL methods. Our theoretical analysis explains the faster and more stabilized optimization of the CFL methods over that of FedAvg, and conjecture their variance reduction effect. In addition, we provide tight convergence rates for standalone CL methods on convex and strongly-convex problems: to the best of our knowledge, we are the first to provide such guarantees for CL on SGD.

- We examine several approximation techniques for CFL framework: the insights therein offer a valuable practical guideline. We demonstrate the efficacy and necessity of CFL methods over the SOTA FL baselines across a range of time-varying heterogeneous data scenarios and datasets.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Federated Learning.** FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2020b) is the de facto standard FL algorithm, in which multiple local SGD steps are executed on the available clients to alleviate the communication bottleneck. While communication efficient, heterogeneity, such as system heterogeneity (Li et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020; Mitra et al., 2021; Diao et al., 2021) and statistical/objective heterogeneity (Li et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020; Mitra et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2020a; Karimireddy et al., 2020b;a), results in inconsistent optimization objectives and drifted clients models, impeding federated optimization considerably. A line of work has been proposed to address the heterogeneity in FL. FedProx (Li et al., 2018) adds the regularization term on the distance of local and global models when performing local training—similar formulations can be found in other recent FL works (Hanzely & Richtárik, 2020; Dinh et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021) for various purposes. To address the issue of objective heterogeneity e.g. caused by heterogeneous data, works like SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2020b; Mitra et al., 2021) introduce the idea of variance reduction on the client local update steps. FedNova (Wang et al., 2020) further proposes a general framework for unifying FedAvg and FedProx, and argues that while averaging, local updates should be normalized to minimize heterogeneity induced by different number of local update steps. However, most of these prior works focus on the fixed heterogeneity across clients and throughout the entire optimization procedure; we instead consider the novel scenario with time-varying data heterogeneity.

The theoretical study on the convergence of FedAvg can date back to the parallel SGD analysis on the identical functions Zinkevich et al. (2010) and recently is improved by Stich (2019); Stich & Karimireddy (2020); Patel & Dieuleveut (2019); Khaled et al. (2020); Woodworth et al. (2020b). For the analysis of heterogeneous data, Li et al. (2020b) first give the convergence rate of FedAvg on non-iid datasets with random selection, assuming that the client optimum are _ϵ_ -close. Woodworth et al. (2020a); Khaled et al. (2020) give tighter convergence rates under the assumption of bounded gradient drift. All above works give a _O_ (1 _/T_ ) convergence rate for convex local objective functions. More recently, Karimireddy et al. (2020b); Koloskova et al. (2020) give the convergence analysis of local SGD for non-convex objective functions under bounded gradient noise assumptions, and obtain a _O_ (1 _/√T_ ) convergence rate. Our theoretical analysis framework covers more challenging timevarying data heterogeneity in FL, which has not been considered in the community yet—our rate can be simplified to the standard FL scenario, matching the tight analysis in Karimireddy et al. (2020b).

**Continual Learning.** Continual learning, also known as incremental learning or lifelong learning, aims to learn from (time-varying) sequential data while avoiding the problem of _catastrophic forgetting_ (French, 1999; Kirkpatrick et al., 2017). There exists a large amount of works, from the perspectives of regularization (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017; Li & Hoiem, 2017; Zenke et al., 2017), experience replay (Castro et al., 2018; Rebuffi et al., 2017), and dynamic architectures (Maltoni & Lomonaco, 2019; Rusu et al., 2016). In this paper, we examine both regularization and experience replay based methods, and compare their empirical performance in depth.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Despite the empirical success, the theoretical analysis of CL is limited: only a very recent preprint (Yin et al., 2020b) provides a viewpoint of regularization-based continual learning, and only for the single worker scenario. In this work, we provide tight convergence analysis for both CL and CFL on SGD.

**Continual Federated Learning.** To our knowledge, the scenario of CFL was originally described in Bui et al. (2018) in order to federated train Bayesian Neural Network and continually learn for Gaussian Process models—it is orthogonal to our optimization aspect in this paper. FedCurv (Shoham et al., 2019) blends EWC (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017) regularization with FedAvg, and empirically shows a faster convergence. FedWeIT (Yoon et al., 2021) extends the regularization idea, with a focus on selective inter-client knowledge transfer for task-adaptive parameters. In a very recent parallel (and empirical) work, CDA-FedAvg (Casado et al., 2021) uses a locally maintained long-term data samples, with asynchronous communication; however, this design choice lacks sufficient numerical results to justify the performance gain (even over FedAvg) and it cannot be applied to more general scenarios. Our theoretically sound CFL framework covers the regularization component of FedCurv and the core set part of CDA-FedAvg, and is orthogonal to the neural architecture manipulation idea in FedWeIT.

## 3 CONTINUAL FEDERATED LEARNING FRAMEWORK

3.1 FORMULATION **Conventional FL Formulation.** The standard FL typically considers a sum-structured distributed optimization problem as below:

where the objective function _f_ ( _**ω**_ ) : R _[d] →_ R is the weighted sum of the local objective functions _fi_ ( _**ω**_ ) := E _Di_ [ _Fi_ ( _**ω**_ )] of _N_ nodes/clients, and _pi_ is the weight of client _i_ . In practice, it may be infeasible to select all clients each round, especially for cross-device setup (McMahan et al., 2017; Kairouz et al., 2019). The standard FedAvg then randomly selects _S_ clients to receive the model parameters from the server ( _S ≤ N_ ) in each communication round and performs _K_ local SGD update steps in the form of _**ω** t,i,k_ = _**ω** t,i,k−_ 1 _− ηl_ ( _∇fi_ ( _**ω** t,i,k−_ 1) + _**ν** t,i,k−_ 1), with local step-size _ηl_ and gradient noise _**ν** t,i,k−_ 1. The selected clients then communicate the updates ∆ _**ω** t,i_ = _**ω** t,i,K −_ _**ω** t_ with the server for the model aggregation: _**ω** t_ +1 = _**ω** t −[η] S[g]_ � _Si_ =1[∆] _**[ω]**[t,i]_[.]

**Continual FL Formulation.** Despite its wide usage, the standard FL formulation given in Equation (1) cannot properly reflect actual time-varying scenarios, such as local datasets changing over time or intermittent clients joining or leaving during training. To address this problem, we propose the Continual Federated Learning (CFL) formulation:

where _ft,i_ ( _**ω**_ ) represents the local objective function of client _i_ at time _t_ . _St_ is a subset of clients sampled from all clients set Ω, where _|St|_ = _S_ . For the time-varying scenarios, client _i_ could have different local objective functions _ft,i_ ( _**ω**_ ) due to the changing local datasets on different _t_ .

3.2 APPROXIMATION OF CFL The challenge of addressing (CFL) formulation stems from the stateless nature of the local datasets, which include unpredictable future datasets, as well as the difficulty of keeping all the previous local datasets. The original definition of (CFL) is theoretically and empirically infeasible. To handle the second case (while ignoring the intractable former), a straightforward approach is to approximate the prior local objective functions (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017; Zenke et al., 2017; Li & Hoiem, 2017), which may be accomplished by retraining information from earlier rounds in accordance with privacy protection standards. Thus, we can express the approximated CFL formulation as:

where _f_[˜] _t,i_ ( _**ω**_ ) denotes the approximated local objective function of client _i_ at time _t_ . In practice, different approximation methods can be used to calculate _f_[˜] _τ,i_ ( _**ω**_ ), and we refer the detailed illustration and discussions of these approximation algorithms in Section 5.1, Section 5.2, and Appendix C.2.2 We give the formal definition of CFL framework in Algorithm 1. CFL methods are a collection of methods that make use of different approximation techniques and are based on Algorithm 1. We retrieve the objective function of conventional Continual Learning (CL), by setting _S_ = 1 in (2). Take note that in most cases, the previous local object functions cannot be properly approximated. Due to the fact that such approximation impairs optimization, we define the information loss below.

3.3 GRADIENT NOISE MODEL

To analyze Equation (2) and Algorithm 1 in-depth, we propose the Gradient Noise Model (Equation (5)) to capture the dynamics of performing SGD with data heterogeneity. We first recap the standard definition of gradient noise in Sammut & Webb (2010); Gower et al. (2019).

**Gradient noise in SGD.** For objective function _f_ ( _**ω**_ ), the gradient with stochastic noise can be defined as _∇f_ ( _**ω**_ ) = _g_ ( _**ω**_ ) + _**ν**_ , where _g_ ( _**ω**_ ) is the stochastic gradient, and _**ν**_ is a zero-mean noise.

**Client drift in FL.** To analyze the impact of data heterogeneity, recent works (Karimireddy et al., 2020b; Khaled et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020b) similarly use the gradient noise to capture the distribution drift between local objective function and global objective function:

_∇f_ ( _**ω**_ ) = _∇fi_ ( _**ω**_ ) + _**δ** i ,_ (4)

where _**δ** i_ is a zero-mean random variable which measures the gradient noise of client _i_ .

**Client drift and time drift in CFL framework.** In (CFL), we use _{t, i}_ pair instead of _i_ to represent client _i_ at time _t_ . Considering the distribution drift in the dimension of client and time, we further modify the gradient noise model in FL as,

_∇f_ ( _**ω**_ ) = _∇ft,i_ ( _**ω**_ ) + _**δ** t,i_ + _**ξ** t,i ._ (5)

Note that we extend the gradient noise to two terms: _**δ** t,i_ and _**ξ** t,i_ . _**δ** t,i_ is a time independent (of _t_ ), zero-mean random variable that measures the drift of client _i_ . The zero-mean _**ξ** t,i_ measures the drift of client _i_ at time _t_ : this value of client _i_ may change over time _t_ , due to the time-varying local datasets. **Remark 3.2.** _We assume different_ _**ξ** t,i are independent to simplify the analysis. We also empirically examine the performance of CFL methods under overlapped time-varying local data in Section 5.2._

## 4 THEORETICAL RESULTS

In this section, we analyze the theoretical performance of Algorithm 1 under Assumption 1–4. We prove that CFL methods converges faster than FedAvg, despite that the term (refer to _ϕt_ in Theorem 4.1) induced by information loss concurrently trades off the optimization. Additionally, our results include the FedAvg rate (and match the prior work) and a novel convergence rate for SGD on CL. In Table 1, we summarize the convergence rate of different algorithms[2] . The proof details refer to Appendix B.4 and Appendix B.5.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

In our numerical investigation, we first use the Noisy Quadratic Model—a simple convex model—to verify our theoretical results stated in Section 4. The results (in Appendix C.1) demonstrate that CFL methods converge much faster than baselines like FedAvg and FedProx, with a smoother convergence curve. These findings are corroborate Remark 4.6 about the variance reduction effect of CFL methods. We further provide extensive empirical evaluations below by comparing CFL methods with various strong FL competitors on different realistic datasets. We explore several approximation techniques in CFL methods and shed light on achieving practical efficient federated learning with time-varying data heterogeneity.

## 5.1 SETUP

**Time-varying heterogeneous local datasets.** We consider federated learn an image classifier using ResNet18 on split-CIFAR10 and split-CIFAR100 datasets, as well as a two layer MLP on the split-Fashion-MNIST dataset. The “split” follows the idea introduced in Yurochkin et al. (2019); Hsu et al. (2019); Reddi et al. (2021), where we leverage the Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) to control the distribution drift with parameter _α_ (See Algorithm 4). Larger _α_ indicates smaller drifts. Unless specifically mentioned otherwise our studies use the following protocol. All datasets are partitioned to 210 subsets for 7 distinct clients: all clients are selected and trained for 500 communication rounds, and each client randomly samples one of the corresponding 30 subsets for the local training— this challenging time-varying scenario mimics the realistic client data sampling scheme (from some underlying distributions). Notably, the training strategy used in this section is applicable to all FL baselines and CFL methods, and we assess the model performance using a global test dataset[3] . We carefully tune the hyper-parameters in all algorithms (details refer to Appendix C.2.1), and report the optimal results (i.e. mean test accuracy across the past 5 best epochs) after repeated trials.

**Approximation techniques in CFL methods.** Below, we review three representative types of information approximation techniques in CL, and use them to investigate the effect of various types of information loss under the (CFL) formulation. We refer to Appendix C.2.2 for a more comprehensive introduction and discussion.

- **Regularization methods.** Instead of keeping datasets from previous rounds, we keep track the gradients and Hessian matrices of local objective functions, and use Taylor Extension to approximate the local objective functions in previous rounds. Note that the trade-off between Hessian estimation and computational overhead constrains the practical feasible of such approach.

- **Core set methods.** Another simple yet effective treatment in CL lines in the category of Exemplar Replay (Rebuffi et al., 2017; Castro et al., 2018). This method selects and regularly saves previous core-set samples (a.k.a. exemplars), and replays them with the current local datasets.

## 5.2 RESULTS

**Comments on different CFL approximation techniques.** In Table 2, we examine the performance of several approximation techniques using the split-CIFAR10 dataset. We can conclude that (1) _Core set methods outperform other methods by a significant margin._ The simple choice of “naive core set”, i.e. randomly and uniformly sample data from the local dataset, surpasses the sampling technique described in iCaRL (Rebuffi et al., 2017)[4] for CL, despite their faster convergence in the initial training phase. (2) _The quality of Hessian estimation matters for regularization based methods._ PyHessian (Yao et al., 2020), as a method to approximate diagonal Hessian matrix, is slightly preferable than Fisher Information Matrix, though the latter one involves less computation. (3) _The performance of generative methods is restricted_ , and we hypothesize that the poor quality of generated samples contributes to the constraint.

In the subsequent evaluation, we consider naive core set sampling for CFL-Core-Set method, and use PyHessian for CFL-Regularization. We exclude the results of generative methods, due to the trivial performance gain and significant computational overhead.

**Understanding various approximated CFL implementations on different datasets.** Table 3 experimentally studies the impacts of various information approximation techniques in CFL methods— as discussed in Section 5.1—and compares them with the backbone algorithm of CFL methods, i.e. FedAvg. We have the following consistent findings on different datasets.

1. _The improvement of CFL methods over FedAvg becomes larger for more challenging tasks_ , as shown in the bottom of the Table 3 for regularization based methods. This might reflect the fact that the precision of the information approximation is more crucial for complicated tasks. Similarly, we demonstrate in Figure 6 (in Appendix C.2.3) that _CFL methods offer better resistance to time-varying non-iid data_ and the significance of such finding is depending on the task difficulty.

2. _For CFL-Regularization method, applying regularization terms on top layers works better than that on all layers._ This observation matches the recent work on decoupling feature extractor and classifier (Collins et al., 2021; Chen & Chao, 2021; Luo et al., 2021): the bottom layers are more generic across tasks and can serve as a global feature extractor, while the top layers are subject to task-specific information.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a Continual Federated Learning (CFL) framework to address time-varying data heterogeneity in federated settings, providing theoretical convergence analysis and empirical validation on split-dataset benchmarks.

The paper makes a compelling attempt to formalize the problem of time-evolving data distributions in federated learning. While the theoretical framework is ambitious, the reliance on strong assumptions regarding information loss and the lack of clarity in the practical implementation of these approximations limit the current impact of the results.

Overview issues:

- Ambiguity in Information Loss Bounding: Assumption 4 bounds the information loss by a constant R, but the paper fails to explain how this bound is derived or verified for the proposed approximation methods (e.g., PyHessian or core-set sampling). Since the convergence rate in Theorem 4.2 depends directly on this R, the theoretical results are difficult to interpret without a clear link between the approximation technique and the magnitude of R. The authors should provide a concrete derivation or empirical estimate of R for at least one of their core-set or regularization methods to show that the assumption is not vacuous.

- Unclear Practicality of Theoretical Convergence Rates: The convergence rates presented in Table 1 and Theorem 4.2 involve several constants (c_A, c_B, c_C, c_D) that are defined based on complex, non-standard parameters. It is difficult to assess whether these rates provide any actual guidance for practitioners or if they are merely artifacts of the specific (and potentially restrictive) assumptions made. A more transparent analysis, perhaps by simplifying the rate for a specific, well-understood scenario, would help readers gauge whether the proposed CFL framework offers a tangible improvement over existing methods in realistic settings.

- Lack of Discussion on Communication Overhead: The paper highlights communication efficiency as a motivation, yet the proposed CFL methods (particularly core-set methods) require storing and potentially transmitting extra information to maintain the approximated local objectives. There is no formal accounting for the additional communication cost per round compared to FedAvg. To be a viable alternative for edge devices, the paper must explicitly quantify the trade-off between the improved convergence rate and the increased communication overhead required to manage the approximated local datasets.

- Inconsistent Handling of Stateless Clients: The paper defines CFL to handle intermittent clients, yet the empirical results in Section 5.2 show that the benefits of core-set methods disappear in the stateless setting (Table 6). This suggests that the framework is highly sensitive to the client statefulness assumption, which is not sufficiently explored in the theoretical section. The authors should clarify the conditions under which the CFL framework is expected to provide gains and explicitly discuss the limitations of their approximation methods when client identity cannot be tracked over time.

- Lack of quantitative communication cost analysis: The paper claims to improve convergence in time-varying settings, but core-set and regularization methods inherently increase the data payload per communication round. While the authors mention communication efficiency as a motivation, they do not provide a formal comparison of the total bits transmitted per round between CFL and FedAvg. To demonstrate that the performance gains are not simply bought by higher communication bandwidth, the authors should include a plot or table showing test accuracy versus total communication cost (in bits or bytes) for a standard task like CIFAR-10.

- Missing sensitivity analysis of the drift parameters: The theoretical bounds in Theorem 4.2 depend on parameters G, D, A, and B, which characterize the client and time drifts. However, the paper does not demonstrate how these bounds behave as the degree of non-stationarity (the 'time drift') increases. A simulation study using the Noisy Quadratic Model, where D and A are systematically varied, would be necessary to verify that the convergence rates actually track with these theoretical constants and to provide intuition on when the CFL framework ceases to be effective.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a concrete mapping between the approximation techniques (e.g., core-set size) and the information loss bound R used in the theoretical analysis.

- Include a formal analysis or empirical measurement of the communication overhead introduced by the CFL approximation methods.

- Clarify the scope of the theoretical results by explicitly stating the regimes where the CFL framework is expected to outperform standard baselines, particularly for stateless clients.

- Simplify the presentation of the convergence rates in Table 1 to make the dependency on key parameters more intuitive for the reader.

comments.title: Inconsistent aggregation formula for FedAvg
comments.quote: The selected clients then communicate the updates $\Delta\bm{\omega}_{t,i}=\bm{\omega}_{t,i,K}-\bm{\omega}_{t}$ with the server for the model aggregation: $\bm{\omega}_{t+1}=\bm{\omega}_{t}-\frac{\eta_{x}}{S}\sum_{i=1}^{S}\Delta\bm{\omega}_{t,i}$.
comments.explanation: The standard FedAvg algorithm defines the global update as a weighted average of local model parameters, typically $\bm{\omega}_{t+1} = \bm{\omega}_t + \frac{1}{S} \sum_{i=1}^S \Delta \bm{\omega}_{t,i}$. Your formula introduces a server learning rate $\eta_x$ and a subtraction operator, which treats the update as a gradient descent step rather than parameter averaging. If you intend to model a variant like FedAvgM, please define $\eta_x$ clearly and correct the sign to match standard literature.

comments.title: Dimensional inconsistency in Assumption 3
comments.quote: Assumption 3 (Bounded gradient drift of CFL framework). Let  $f(\omega) = \nabla f_{t,i}(\omega) + \delta_{t,i} + \xi_{t,i}$ , where  $\delta_{t,i}$  measures client drift and  $\xi_{t,i}$  indicates the time drift of  $\{t,i\}$ .
comments.explanation:
The definition $f(\omega) = 
abla f_{t,i}(\omega) + \delta_{t,i} + \xi_{t,i}$ is mathematically ill-posed because $f(\omega)$ is a scalar objective function, while the right-hand side is a vector-valued gradient. Please rewrite this as $
abla f_{t,i}(\omega) = 
abla f(\omega) + \delta_{t,i} + \xi_{t,i}$ to ensure the dimensions match and the drift terms are correctly centered around the global gradient.

comments.title: Ill-posed objective function due to lack of normalization
comments.quote: $f^{*}=\min_{\bm{\omega}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}}\left[f(\bm{\omega}):=\sum_{t=1}^{T}\sum_{i\in\mathcal{S}_{t}}p_{t,i}f_{t,i}(\bm{\omega})\right]\,,$ (CFL)
comments.explanation: The objective function $f(\omega)$ is defined as a sum over $T$ time steps without normalization. As $T$ increases, the magnitude of $f(\omega)$ and its gradient scale linearly with $T$, which prevents convergence unless the learning rate is decayed at a rate proportional to $1/T$. To ensure the objective remains stationary as the time horizon grows, please normalize the sum by $1/T$.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a Continual Federated Learning (CFL) framework that approximates past local objectives to handle time-varying data heterogeneity. While the motivation addresses a realistic gap in standard FL, the theoretical bounds are overly complex and obscure the claimed convergence advantage. The experimental validation does not directly measure the core theoretical constructs, and key baselines are excluded without sufficient justification. Adding computational analysis and engaging with non-stationary FL literature would strengthen the work.

This paper tackles a practical gap in federated learning by proposing a framework that approximates past objectives to handle time-varying data heterogeneity. The theoretical extension of gradient noise models and the empirical comparison across approximation strategies show promise. However, the mathematical bounds obscure the claimed advantages, and the experimental validation does not directly measure the theoretical constructs.

Overview issues:

- Convergence bounds obscure the claimed advantage over FedAvg: The theoretical results in Theorems 4.2 and 4.5, along with Table 1, present convergence rates filled with loosely defined composite constants ($c_A, c_B, c_C, c_O$, etc.). The paper claims CFL converges faster than FedAvg due to variance reduction, but the bounds for both methods contain fundamentally different terms and constants. Without simplifying these expressions or providing a direct term-by-term comparison, it is impossible to verify whether CFL’s rate is actually tighter or merely structurally different. Readers will wonder if the apparent improvement stems from the approximation framework or simply from different bounding techniques. The analysis would benefit from a simplified rate under a specific distribution shift model, or a numerical comparison of the bound values across varying $R$ and $D$.

- Theoretical constructs lack empirical validation: The framework relies heavily on time drift ($\xi_{t,i}$) and information loss ($R$) to drive the theoretical guarantees, yet the experiments never measure or report these quantities. Section 5.1 describes an LDA-based partitioning scheme to simulate changing data distributions, but this setup does not directly correspond to the mathematical definitions of drift or approximation error used in Section 3.3 and Assumption 4. Because the paper does not track how approximation quality changes during training or correlate it with the convergence speed, the theoretical trade-off between approximation error and optimization performance remains unverified. Adding metrics that compute the actual gradient discrepancy or replay fidelity during training, and plotting them against convergence curves, would ground the theoretical claims.

- Dismissing established non-IID FL baselines requires justification: Section 5.2 explicitly excludes SCAFFOLD and Ditto from the comparison, stating they are infeasible to be applied in continual scenarios. This assertion lacks technical support. SCAFFOLD is explicitly designed to correct client drift caused by heterogeneous data, and Ditto addresses non-IID distributions through personalization. Both methods can be adapted to settings where data distributions shift across rounds, or at least warrant a discussion of their failure modes in this context. Excluding them without implementation or a rigorous argument weakens the empirical validation of CFL. The authors should either implement adapted versions of these baselines or provide a clear theoretical explanation of why their update rules diverge under time-varying objectives.

- Missing computational and memory complexity analysis: The proposed CFL framework requires clients to approximate past local objectives using regularization terms or core-set replay. For deep neural networks, maintaining Hessian approximations or storing exemplar buffers per client introduces significant memory overhead and computational costs that scale with model size and dataset history. The paper provides no analysis of these costs, nor does it report wall-clock time, memory usage, or communication overhead in the experiments. Without this information, it is unclear whether the theoretical convergence gains translate to practical efficiency, especially in cross-device settings where resources are constrained. A dedicated subsection detailing the space and time complexity of each approximation method, alongside runtime benchmarks, is necessary to evaluate practical feasibility.

- Positioning overlooks prior work on non-stationary federated learning: The abstract and introduction claim this is the first theoretical study of time-varying FL, yet the literature contains substantial work on non-stationary FL, distribution shift, and continual learning in distributed settings. The formulation in Equation (CFL) essentially captures changing non-IID data, which overlaps significantly with existing research on FL under distribution drift. By not engaging with this prior work, the paper risks overstating its novelty and missing opportunities to position CFL within the broader context of adaptive optimization. The authors should explicitly differentiate their approach from non-stationary FL methods, cite relevant prior theoretical analyses, and clarify whether the CFL framework offers distinct advantages over existing drift-handling techniques.

- Concrete parametric example to calibrate drift and information loss bounds: The convergence analysis in Theorems 4.1 and 4.2 introduces composite constants that bundle client drift, time drift, and information loss. Without a fully specified parametric model, it is difficult to gauge how these terms interact or how the approximation quality $R$ quantitatively affects the convergence rate. The paper would benefit from computing the bounds for a simple quadratic objective with explicitly defined drift parameters. This would allow readers to see exactly how the rate simplifies when $R$ is small versus large, and verify the claimed faster convergence over FedAvg in a tractable setting.

- Practical guidance for selecting the historical weighting scheme $p_{\tau,i}$: Theorem 4.2 prescribes an optimal weighting scheme $p_{t,i} = \frac{(t-1)R^2 + D^2}{tD^2 + (t-1)R^2}$ to achieve the derived convergence rate. The experiments in Section 5 do not explain how this weighting is implemented in practice, nor do they discuss how to estimate the unknown drift bound $D$ and information loss bound $R$ required to compute it. The authors mention using naive core set sampling and PyHessian but omit the mechanism for setting the historical weights in Algorithm 1. A discussion on practical heuristics for choosing $p_{\tau,i}$, or an ablation study showing sensitivity to different weight schedules, is necessary to bridge the theory and the reported experiments.

- Empirical comparison to methods claimed to be subsumed by CFL: The Related Work section asserts that the CFL framework covers the regularization component of FedCurv and the core set component of CDA-FedAvg. Despite these claims, the empirical evaluation in Section 5 never includes FedCurv or CDA-FedAvg as baselines. To substantiate the claim that CFL unifies or improves upon these approaches, the authors should either implement them as special cases of the CFL framework or include them in the comparison tables. Without this, the assertion that CFL subsumes prior work remains unverified and leaves a gap between the theoretical positioning and the empirical validation.

- Non-convex convergence analysis lacks corresponding empirical verification: Theorem 4.2 derives a convergence rate for non-convex objectives based on the average squared gradient norm converging to $\epsilon$. The experimental setup in Section 5 evaluates classification accuracy on ResNet18 and MLP models, which are inherently non-convex. The paper does not report gradient norm trajectories or verify that the non-convex rate holds empirically. To fully deliver on the theoretical claims, the authors should include an ablation or supplementary experiment tracking the average gradient norm over rounds for the neural network setups, or explicitly acknowledge that the non-convex theoretical results are not numerically corroborated in the current experiments.

Recommendation: Major revision. The convergence analysis requires substantial simplification and direct comparison to validate the core claim. The empirical section must measure theoretical quantities, include key baselines, and report computational overhead to support practical applicability.

Revision targets:

- Simplify the convergence bounds and provide a direct, term-by-term comparison between CFL and FedAvg rates to substantiate the claimed variance reduction.

- Implement empirical metrics to measure time drift and approximation error during training, and correlate these values with convergence performance.

- Add SCAFFOLD and Ditto to the baseline comparison, or provide a rigorous theoretical explanation of their inapplicability in time-varying settings.

- Include a detailed analysis of memory and computational overhead for each approximation method, alongside runtime benchmarks.

- Reframe the novelty claims to explicitly engage with and differentiate from existing non-stationary FL and distribution shift literature.

comments.title: Algorithm uses true gradients instead of approximated gradients
comments.quote: ĝt,i,k(ωt,i,k-1) = (pt,i∇ft,i(ωt,i,k-1) + ∑τ=1t-1pτ,i∇fτ,i(ωt,i,k-1)) + νt,i,k.  |
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 computes gradients using the true historical objective functions $f_{\tau,i}$, which directly contradicts the stateless CFL premise where past data is unavailable and must be approximated. Line 6 should use the approximated gradients $\nabla \hat{f}_{\tau,i}(\omega_{t,i,k-1})$ to align with Equation (2). This change is necessary for the algorithm to actually implement the proposed method.

comments.title: Incorrect sign in Assumption 1 corollary
comments.quote:
$ rac{1}{2L}\| 
abla f_{t,i}(\mathbf{x}) - 
abla f_{t,i}(\mathbf{y})\|^2 \leq f_{t,i}(\mathbf{x}) - f_{t,i}(\mathbf{y}) - 
abla f_{t,i}(\mathbf{x})^T (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y})$
comments.explanation: For a convex function, the right-hand side is non-positive, while the left-hand side is a squared norm and thus non-negative. The inequality only holds trivially. The standard co-coercivity inequality requires the function values to be ordered as $f_{t,i}(\mathbf{y}) - f_{t,i}(\mathbf{x}) - \nabla f_{t,i}(\mathbf{x})^T (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x})$. Correcting this sign error is essential for the subsequent smoothness assumptions.

comments.title: Dimensional mismatch in Assumption 3 definition
comments.quote: Let  $f(\omega) = \nabla f_{t,i}(\omega) + \delta_{t,i} + \xi_{t,i}$
comments.explanation:
The left-hand side $f(\omega)$ denotes a scalar objective function, while the right-hand side sums a gradient vector $
abla f_{t,i}(\omega)$ with drift terms. This is a typographical error in the gradient decomposition. The authors likely intended $
abla f_{t,i}(\omega) = 
abla f(\omega) + \delta_{t,i} + \xi_{t,i}$. Clarifying this vector-scalar mismatch is necessary before the drift bounds can be meaningfully applied.

comments.title: Zero-mean claim contradicts standard definition of client drift
comments.quote: $\delta_{t,i}$  is a time independent (of  $t$ ), zero-mean random variable that measures the drift of client  $i$
comments.explanation:
In federated learning, client drift is typically defined as the deterministic bias $
abla f(\omega) - \mathbb{E}[
abla f_i(\omega)]$, which captures systematic data heterogeneity and is generally non-zero. Claiming $\delta_{t,i}$ is zero-mean implies $\mathbb{E}[
abla f_i(\omega)] = 
abla f(\omega)$, which eliminates the very heterogeneity the model aims to capture. If $\delta_{t,i}$ is intended to be stochastic noise, it should be distinguished from the systematic drift component.

comments.title: Undefined composite constants in Table 1

$c_{\mu_B} = 1 + B^2 +A^2,c_A = \frac{D^2B^2}{B^2 + D^2},c_B = \frac{D^6}{(R^2 + D^2)^2},N$  is the number of chosen clients

Specifically, $c_{PB}$, $c_C$, $c_D$, $\rho$, $K_1$, and $F$ appear in the convergence rate rows but lack definitions. Additionally, there is a notation inconsistency: the footnote defines $c_{\mu_B}$ (with subscript $\mu_B$), while the table uses $c_{\mu B}$ (space instead of subscript). comments.title: Vague definition of constant c_m in non-convex rate
comments.quote: $c_{m}$ is a constant related to $A$, $B$ and $R$, and $F=\|\omega_{0}-\omega^{*}\|^{2}$.
comments.explanation: The convergence rate in Equation (9) depends on $c_m$, but it is only vaguely described as 'a constant related to A, B and R'. In theoretical guarantees, all constants in the big-O bound must be explicitly defined or bounded in terms of the problem parameters ($L, \mu, \sigma, G, D, R$, etc.) to make the rate verifiable. Rewrite '$c_{m}$ is a constant related to $A$, $B$ and $R$' with an explicit formula or bound in terms of $A, B, R$ to make the rate verifiable.

comments.title: Conflation of approximation error and gradient noise
comments.quote: rem 4.2). The upper bound $R$ is linearly correlated to gradient noise, where $
comments.explanation: The authors claim $R$ (the bound on information loss $\|\Delta_{t,i}(\omega)\| \leq R$) is "linearly correlated to gradient noise." However, $R$ quantifies model drift or approximation error between local and global objectives, while gradient noise is independently bounded by variance $\sigma^2$ (Assumption 2) and drift $G^2$ (Assumption 3). There is no mathematical derivation linking $R$ to stochastic gradient noise. This conflation obscures the distinct roles of approximation error and sampling noise in the convergence analysis.

comments.title: Contradiction between text claim and Table 4 results
comments.quote: We find that CFL-Regularization methods consistently outperform alternative FL baselines, similar to the observations in Table 4.
comments.explanation: Table 4 shows that at α=0.1, CFL-Regularization achieves 70.86% accuracy, which is lower than FedProx (71.57%) and CFL-Regularization + FedProx (71.50%). Therefore, the claim that it 'consistently outperforms alternative FL baselines' is contradicted by the data presented in Table 4. The authors should correct the text to accurately reflect the results, or clarify that the outperformance holds only for specific baselines or at higher α.

comments.title: Abstract overstates alignment between theory and experiments
comments.quote: In a series of experiments, we show that the numerical findings match the convergence analysis, and CFL methods significantly outperform the other SOTA FL baselines.
comments.explanation: The abstract asserts that numerical findings match the convergence analysis. This claim does not hold given the paper's actual setup. The theoretical results derive convergence rates based on the average squared gradient norm for non-convex objectives. The experiments, however, only report classification accuracy on ResNet18 and MLP models. Because the paper does not track gradient norm trajectories or provide a direct mapping between accuracy gains and the derived gradient-based bounds, readers cannot verify the stated match. The authors should either include gradient norm metrics in the experiments to substantiate the theoretical claims or revise the abstract to state that experiments validate practical performance rather than directly verifying the convergence rates.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a Continual Federated Learning (CFL) framework to handle time-varying heterogeneous data in FL. The core idea—extending FL to account for temporal drift—is timely and relevant. However, the paper suffers from several critical issues: the theoretical analysis relies on assumptions that are unrealistic for the claimed setting, the empirical evaluation lacks rigorous baselines and statistical validation, and the practical feasibility of the framework is not adequately addressed.

The paper tackles an important and underexplored problem—time-varying heterogeneity in federated learning. Its main strength is the formalization of a CFL framework that explicitly models client and time drifts. However, the theoretical results are built on assumptions that are either too strong or not validated, the empirical comparisons are incomplete (missing key baselines like SCAFFOLD, FedCurv, and CDA-FedAvg), and the experimental design does not convincingly isolate the effect of temporal dynamics from other factors. The contribution, while novel in framing, is not sufficiently substantiated for a top venue.

Overview issues:

- Unrealistic assumptions in the theoretical analysis undermine the claimed results: The convergence analysis (Section 4) relies on Assumptions 1–4, several of which are problematic for the claimed setting of time-evolving heterogeneous data. Assumption 3 assumes that the client drift δ and time drift ξ are zero-mean and have bounded variance, but in practice, temporal drifts are often systematic (e.g., concept drift) and not zero-mean. Assumption 4 bounds the information loss by an arbitrary constant R, yet for many approximation methods (e.g., core sets), the error depends on the data distribution and can grow with time. Moreover, the analysis assumes that local objective functions are L-smooth and μ-convex (Assumption 1), which excludes the non-convex neural networks used in experiments. The paper claims a faster convergence rate for CFL over FedAvg, but this advantage is expressed in terms of constants (c_A, c_B) that depend on R, D, and G, which are not measurable in practice. Without a way to verify these constants, the theoretical improvement is not actionable. The authors should either relax these assumptions to match realistic conditions or provide empirical validation of the assumptions on real data.

- Empirical evaluation lacks proper baselines and statistical rigor: The experimental section (Section 5) compares CFL methods against only FedAvg, FedProx, and MimeLite. Key baselines designed for continual federated learning or time-varying data—such as FedCurv (Shoham et al., 2019), CDA-FedAvg (Casado et al., 2021), and FedWeIT (Yoon et al., 2021)—are mentioned in related work but not included in the comparisons. The paper claims that SCAFFOLD and Ditto are 'infeasible' for the continual scenario, but this is not justified—SCAFFOLD's control variates could be adapted to track temporal changes. Furthermore, the results report only mean accuracy over 'repeated trials' without specifying the number of trials or providing statistical significance tests (e.g., confidence intervals, paired t-tests). The standard deviations are small, but without knowing the number of seeds, the reader cannot assess reliability. The authors should include a comprehensive set of baselines, report results over at least 5 random seeds, and perform statistical tests to support claims of superiority.

- The CFL formulation conflates client and time dimensions, obscuring the actual contribution: The CFL formulation in Equation (CFL) sums over time t and clients i, but the practical implementation (Algorithm 1) approximates past objectives using a single gradient term that combines both dimensions. The gradient noise model (Equation 5) decomposes drift into client-specific (δ) and time-specific (ξ) components, but the analysis does not actually separate their effects—the convergence bounds treat them symmetrically. This conflates two distinct phenomena: cross-client heterogeneity (which existing FL methods address) and temporal non-stationarity (which is the claimed novelty). The paper would benefit from a clearer decomposition: for example, isolating scenarios where only time drift exists (all clients have identical evolving distributions) versus only client drift exists (static heterogeneity). Without such ablation, it is unclear whether the CFL framework's advantage comes from handling temporal dynamics or simply from better approximation of past data.

- The information loss concept is not operationalized, making the theory untestable: Definition 3.1 defines information loss as the difference between true and approximated gradients, but the paper never shows how to compute or bound this quantity for any real approximation method. Assumption 4 assumes an arbitrary bound R, but Lemma B.3 only provides a bound for Taylor-expansion-based regularization under strong assumptions (e.g., Hessian boundedness). For core set methods, the information loss depends on the sampling strategy and core set size, yet no formula or bound is given. Figure 1 plots accuracy against core set size but does not actually measure information loss—it merely shows that larger core sets improve accuracy. Without a computable definition, the theoretical results (e.g., Theorem 4.2) cannot be validated or falsified. The authors should provide explicit bounds for each approximation technique used in experiments, or at least a method to estimate information loss from data.

- The experimental design does not convincingly demonstrate handling of time-varying data: The paper constructs time-varying datasets by partitioning data into 210 subsets for 7 clients, with each client randomly sampling one subset per round. This setup simulates data rotation, not gradual concept drift or evolving distributions. The clients see completely different data each round, which is an extreme form of non-stationarity. In contrast, real-world time-varying scenarios often involve gradual shifts (e.g., seasonal trends) or recurring patterns. The paper does not test such gradual drifts. Moreover, the overlap experiments (Table 5) vary the overlap percentage but keep the total dataset size fixed, which does not model data accumulation or deletion. The authors should include experiments with controlled concept drift (e.g., rotating MNIST, permuted CIFAR) and measure forgetting (e.g., backward transfer) to demonstrate that CFL mitigates catastrophic forgetting, a key claim of continual learning.

- The convergence rate comparison in Table 1 is misleading due to incomparable terms: Table 1 compares convergence rates across algorithms using different constants (G, D, A, B, R, etc.), but these constants have different meanings for different methods. For FedAvg, G and D bound gradient drift; for CFL, additional constants A and R appear. The paper claims CFL achieves faster rates because terms like c_A = G^2 + (D^2 R^2)/(R^2 + D^2) are smaller than G^2 + D^2 for FedAvg. However, R is an upper bound on information loss that can be arbitrarily large if approximation is poor. The rate for CFL includes additional terms like sqrt(c_B/μ^2 ε) and cbrt(c_B/μ^2 ε) that do not appear in FedAvg's rate. For small ε, these extra terms can dominate, making CFL slower. The paper does not discuss this trade-off. A fair comparison would require specifying how R, A, B, D, and G relate to problem parameters (e.g., data dimensionality, number of clients) and showing regimes where CFL is provably faster.

- No concrete example showing CFL succeeds where FedAvg fails: The paper claims CFL methods handle time-varying heterogeneity better than FedAvg, but it never demonstrates a specific, fully-specified data-generating process (DGP) where FedAvg provably fails and CFL succeeds. For a testable restriction like the convergence rate improvement, the paper should construct a simple parametric example — for instance, a quadratic model with a known time-varying mean — compute the exact convergence rates for both FedAvg and CFL, and show numerically that the predicted advantage materializes. Without such a worked example, the theoretical claims remain abstract and untestable. The authors should add a section or appendix that computes the one-round progress (Theorem 4.1) for a concrete two-client, two-time-period model and verifies the variance reduction effect.

- Missing simulation study with controlled concept drift: The experimental design uses data rotation (each client samples a new subset each round) rather than gradual concept drift or recurring patterns. This extreme non-stationarity does not match real-world scenarios like seasonal trends or user preference shifts. The paper should include a simulation with a known drift process — for example, a rotating MNIST benchmark where the label distribution shifts smoothly over rounds — and measure both accuracy and forgetting (backward transfer). This would directly test whether CFL mitigates catastrophic forgetting, a core claim. Without such an experiment, the empirical evaluation does not convincingly demonstrate that CFL handles time-evolving data better than standard FL methods.

- Information loss is not computed for any approximation method: Definition 3.1 defines information loss as the gradient difference, but the paper never computes or bounds this quantity for the core set or regularization methods used in experiments. Figure 1 plots accuracy against core set size but does not actually measure information loss — it merely shows correlation. For the theory to be falsifiable and useful, the authors should provide explicit bounds for each approximation technique. For example, for naive core set sampling, they could bound the expected information loss in terms of the core set size and the data distribution. Without this, the theoretical results (e.g., Theorem 4.2) cannot be validated or applied in practice.

- No discussion of how to estimate key constants in practice: The convergence rates depend on unobservable constants G, D, A, B, R that bound gradient drift and information loss. The paper never discusses how a practitioner could estimate these quantities from data or whether they can be bounded by observable statistics (e.g., gradient norms, loss values). For a paper that claims practical relevance, this is a notable gap. The authors should add a paragraph in Section 4 or Appendix B explaining how to estimate or bound these constants in realistic federated settings, or at least acknowledge the limitation and suggest heuristic proxies. Without such guidance, the theoretical improvement is not actionable.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper addresses an important problem and proposes a reasonable framework, but the theoretical analysis rests on unrealistic assumptions, the empirical evaluation is incomplete, and the claimed advantages are not convincingly demonstrated. A major revision should address the unrealistic assumptions, include proper baselines, and provide experiments that isolate the effect of temporal dynamics.

Revision targets:

- Relax or empirically validate Assumptions 3 and 4 for real-world time-varying data; provide computable bounds on information loss for each approximation method used.

- Include comprehensive baselines (FedCurv, CDA-FedAvg, FedWeIT, and an adapted SCAFFOLD) and report results over at least 5 random seeds with statistical significance tests.

- Design experiments with gradual concept drift (e.g., rotating MNIST, permuted CIFAR) and measure forgetting (backward transfer) to directly test the continual learning aspect.

- Clarify the decomposition of client and time drifts in the theoretical analysis, and provide ablation studies that isolate the effect of temporal dynamics from cross-client heterogeneity.

- Revise the convergence rate comparison (Table 1) to include the extra terms for CFL and discuss regimes where CFL is provably faster than FedAvg.

comments.title: Assumption 3 zero-mean time drift contradicts systematic temporal shifts
comments.quote: We assume  $\mathbb{E}[\delta |\omega] = 0$ ,  $\mathbb{E}[\xi |\omega] = 0$
comments.explanation: The assumption that the time drift ξ has zero mean is inconsistent with the paper's own description of time-varying heterogeneity. The abstract and introduction emphasize that local datasets 'vary over time' and that 'intermittent clients can join or depart during training without prior notice.' Such temporal changes are typically systematic (e.g., concept drift, distribution shift) and not zero-mean. A zero-mean drift would imply that the gradient perturbations cancel out over time, which contradicts the notion of a persistent shift. The authors should either justify why zero-mean drift is a reasonable approximation for the scenarios they consider, or replace Assumption 3 with a more realistic model that allows for non-zero mean drift. Without this, the theoretical results may not apply to the claimed practical settings.

comments.title: Information loss bound R is not operationalized
comments.quote: Assumption 4 (Bounded information loss). We assume the information loss  $\Delta_{t,i}(\omega)$  can be bounded by an arbitrary non-negative value  $R$ , i.e.  $\| \Delta_{t,i}(\omega)\| \leq R$ .
comments.explanation: Assumption 4 assumes a uniform bound R on the information loss, but the paper never shows how to compute or estimate R for any concrete approximation method used in experiments (e.g., core sets, regularization). The bound is introduced as an arbitrary constant, making the theoretical results (e.g., Theorem 4.2) untestable and non-falsifiable. The authors should provide explicit bounds for each approximation technique they employ, or at least discuss how R relates to problem parameters such as core set size, data dimensionality, or number of clients. Without this, the claimed convergence advantage of CFL over FedAvg cannot be verified in practice.

comments.title: Type error in Assumption 3 definition
comments.quote: Assumption 3 (Bounded gradient drift of CFL framework). Let  $f(\omega) = \nabla f_{t,i}(\omega) + \delta_{t,i} + \xi_{t,i}$
comments.explanation:
The equation $f(\omega) = 
abla f_{t,i}(\omega) + \delta_{t,i} + \xi_{t,i}$ is dimensionally inconsistent: $f(\omega)$ is a scalar (function value), while the right-hand side is a vector (gradient plus drift terms). This is a clear typo. The intended statement is almost certainly $
abla f(\omega) = 
abla f_{t,i}(\omega) + \delta_{t,i} + \xi_{t,i}$, as the subsequent bounds on $\|\delta\|^2$ and $\|\xi\|^2$ are vector norms. Rewrite the defining equation as $
abla f(\omega) = 
abla f_{t,i}(\omega) + \delta_{t,i} + \xi_{t,i}$ to resolve the type mismatch.

comments.title: Inconsistent index in the approximated CFL formulation
comments.quote: $\hat{f}_{t}^{*}=\min_{\bm{\omega}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}}\left[\hat{f}_{t}(\bm{\omega}):=\sum_{i\in\mathcal{S}_{t}}p_{t,i}f_{t,i}(\bm{\omega})+\sum_{\tau=1}^{t-1}\sum_{i\in\mathcal{S}_{t}}p_{\tau,i}\hat{f}_{\tau,i}(\bm{\omega})\right]\,,$ (2)
comments.explanation: The second sum in (2) runs over $i \in \mathcal{S}_t$, the set of clients active at time $t$, but it sums approximated objectives $\hat{f}_{\tau,i}(\bm{\omega})$ from earlier times $\tau < t$. If a client $i$ was active at time $\tau$ but is not in $\mathcal{S}_t$, its past objective is omitted; conversely, if a client $i$ is in $\mathcal{S}_t$ but was not active at time $\tau$, the term $\hat{f}_{\tau,i}$ is undefined. The index set should be $\bigcup_{\tau=1}^{t-1} \mathcal{S}_\tau$ (or a union over all clients that have ever participated) to make the sum well-defined. Rewrite the second sum as $\sum_{\tau=1}^{t-1} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{S}_\tau} p_{\tau,i} \hat{f}_{\tau,i}(\bm{\omega})$.

comments.title: Definition 3.1 uses inconsistent notation for approximated objective
comments.quote:
finition 3.1 (Information Loss). We define the information loss as the difference between the approximated local objective function  $\tilde{f}_{t,i}(\omega)$  and the real local objective function  $f_{t,i}(\omega)$ , i.e.

$$
\Delta_ {t, i} (\boldsymbol {\omega}) = \nabla f _ {t, i} (\boldsymbol {\omega}) - \nabla \tilde {f} _ {t, i} (\boldsymbol {\omega}). \tag {3}
$$
comments.explanation:
The definition text says the information loss is the difference between the *functions* $	ilde{f}_{t,i}$ and $f_{t,i}$, but equation (3) defines it as the difference between their *gradients*. These are not the same: the function difference is $f_{t,i}(\omega) - 	ilde{f}_{t,i}(\omega)$, while the gradient difference is $
abla f_{t,i}(\omega) - 
abla 	ilde{f}_{t,i}(\omega)$. The paper later uses the gradient difference (e.g., in Assumption 4 and Theorem 4.1), so the text should match the equation. Rewrite the definition text as "the difference between the gradients of the approximated and real local objective functions" or change (3) to use $f$ instead of $
abla f$ and adjust all subsequent uses accordingly.

comments.title: Notation conflict in Equation (5): δ_{t,i} is time-independent but has t subscript
comments.explanation: The first-pass reviewer correctly identifies that δ_{t,i} is described as 'time independent (of t)' yet carries a t subscript. This is confusing because the subscript t normally indicates dependence on t. The authors likely intend δ_i (as in Equation (4)) to represent a time-invariant client drift, and the t subscript in (5) is a notational oversight. Rewrite δ_{t,i} as δ_i in Equation (5) and the surrounding text to maintain consistency with the definition of client drift as time-independent.

comments.title: Independence assumption for ξ_{t,i} is unrealistic for temporal data
comments.quote: Remark 3.2. We assume different  $\xi_{t,i}$  are independent to simplify the analysis.
comments.explanation: The first-pass reviewer correctly notes that temporal data often exhibit correlation across time steps (e.g., gradual drift, seasonality). The paper acknowledges this is a simplification but does not justify it or discuss its impact. This is a genuine limitation: convergence bounds derived under independence may not hold under realistic temporal dependence. The authors should either provide a justification (e.g., that the time scale of drift is much longer than the SGD step size, making consecutive ξ_{t,i} approximately independent) or analyze the effect of correlation (e.g., via mixing conditions).

comments.title: Equation (5) lacks a definition of ∇f_{t,i}(ω)
comments.explanation: The paper introduces ∇f_{t,i}(ω) as the gradient of the local objective for client i at time t, but does not formally define it. In standard FL, the local objective f_i is defined over client i's static dataset. For time-evolving data, the local dataset changes over time, so f_{t,i} must be defined explicitly (e.g., as the expected loss over the data distribution at time t). Without this definition, Equation (5) is ambiguous. The authors should add a formal definition of f_{t,i} before or after Equation (5).

comments.title: Unjustified substitution in corollary derivation
comments.quote: We can derive the corollary from Assumption 3 that  $\mathbb{E}\left[\|\pmb{\xi}\|^2|\pmb{\omega}\right] \leq \hat{D}^2 + \hat{A}^2 G^2 + \hat{A}^2 B^2 \mathbb{E}\|\nabla f(\pmb{\omega})\|^2$ , which can be simplified to  $\mathbb{E}\left[\|\pmb{\xi}\|^2|\pmb{\omega}\right] \leq D^2 + A^2 \mathbb{E}\|\nabla f(\pmb{\omega})\|^2$ , by assuming  $A^2 = \hat{A}^2 B^2$ ,  $D^2 = \hat{D}^2 + \hat{A}^2 G^2$ .
comments.explanation:
The derivation claims that $\mathbb{E}\|
abla f_i(\omega)\|^2 \leq G^2 + B^2 \mathbb{E}\|
abla f(\omega)\|^2$, but Assumption 3 only provides a bound on $\mathbb{E}[\|\delta\|^2|\omega]$, not directly on $\mathbb{E}\|
abla f_i(\omega)\|^2$. The step that substitutes $\mathbb{E}\|
abla f_i(\omega)\|^2$ with $G^2 + B^2 \mathbb{E}\|
abla f(\omega)\|^2$ is not justified by the stated assumptions. The paper claims equivalence to the $(G,B)$ gradient drift assumption, but that equivalence is not proven here. The authors should provide a clear chain of inequalities showing how the bound on $\mathbb{E}[\|\xi\|^2|\omega]$ follows from the given bounds, or state the needed bound on $\mathbb{E}\|
abla f_i(\omega)\|^2$ as an additional condition.

comments.title: Table 1 FedAvg rate includes unexplained constant
comments.quote: | Ours | $\frac{\sigma^2}{\mu NK\epsilon} + \frac{G^2+D^2}{\mu\epsilon} + \frac{c_{PB}}{\mu}$ | $\frac{\sigma^2}{NK\epsilon^2} + \frac{G^2+D^2}{\epsilon^2} + \frac{G+D}{\epsilon}$ |
comments.explanation: The table entry for FedAvg (Ours) in the strongly convex column includes the term $\frac{c_{PB}}{\mu}$, where $c_{PB} = 1 + B^2 + A^2$. However, Theorem 4.5 (the convergence rate for FedAvg under time-varying scenarios) does not contain any term involving $A$ or $B$ in its stated rate — it only depends on $G$ and $D$. The constant $c_{PB}$ appears in the CFL and CL rows but not in the FedAvg row of the theorem statement. This suggests either the table entry is incorrect (it should not include $c_{PB}$) or the theorem statement is missing terms. The authors should reconcile the table with Theorem 4.5: either remove $c_{PB}/\mu$ from the FedAvg strongly convex rate or update the theorem to include it.

comments.title: Arbitrary R makes convergence rate potentially vacuous
comments.quote: Assumption 4 (Bounded information loss). We assume the information loss  $\Delta_{t,i}(\omega)$  can be bounded by an arbitrary non-negative value  $R$ , i.e.  $\| \Delta_{t,i}(\omega)\| \leq R$ .
comments.explanation: The assumption states that the information loss is bounded by an arbitrary constant $R$, but the convergence theorems (e.g., Theorem 4.2) use $R$ in the definition of $c_B = D^6/(R^2 + D^2)^2$. Since $R$ is arbitrary, the convergence rate can be made arbitrarily fast by choosing $R$ large (making $c_B$ small). However, in practice, $R$ is not a free parameter — it is determined by the approximation method and the data. The assumption does not provide any guidance on how $R$ relates to problem parameters, making the claimed improvement over FedAvg (which does not involve $R$) potentially vacuous. The authors should either provide a bound on $R$ in terms of observable quantities (e.g., core set size, regularization strength) or clarify that $R$ is a problem-dependent constant that cannot be arbitrarily tuned.

comments.title: Forward reference for p_{τ,i} in Theorem 4.1
comments.explanation: The definition of c_R in Theorem 4.1 uses p_{τ,i} which are only specified later in Theorem 4.2. This forward reference makes the bound in Theorem 4.1 ambiguous at the point of reading. The authors should either define p_{τ,i} before Theorem 4.1 or restructure the presentation so that c_R is defined after the weights are introduced. Rewrite Theorem 4.1 to either include the definition of p_{τ,i} or defer the explicit form of c_R to after the weights are defined.

comments.title: φ_t uses undefined ω_0
comments.quote: $\varphi_{t}=\mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{\tau=1}^{t-1}p_{t,i}R\left\|\omega_{0}-\omega^{*}\right\|\right)$.
comments.explanation: The expression for φ_t involves ‖ω_0 - ω*‖, but ω_0 is not defined in this section or earlier in the paper. The paper defines ω* as the minimizer of f(ω) = (1/T) ∑_{t=1}^T (1/N) ∑_{i=1}^N f_{t,i}(ω), but ω_0 is never introduced. This makes the bound on φ_t unverifiable. The authors should define ω_0 explicitly, e.g., as the initial model parameter before any training round.

comments.title: c_m in non-convex rate lacks explicit definition
comments.quote: c_{m}$ is a constant related to $A$, $B$ and $R$,
comments.explanation: In the non-convex convergence rate (9), c_m appears in the denominator of the first and third terms, but c_m is only described as 'a constant related to A, B and R' without any explicit formula or bound. Without a concrete definition, the rate cannot be evaluated or compared. The authors should provide an explicit expression for c_m, or at least state its dependence on the problem parameters (e.g., c_m = 1/(1 + A^2 + B^2) or similar).

comments.title: c_{A'} in Remark 4.4 appears to have a typo
comments.explanation: In Remark 4.4, c_{A'} is given as (G^2 + G^2) R^2 / (G^2 + D^2 + R^2). The numerator contains G^2 + G^2, which simplifies to 2G^2 R^2. This seems like a typo — likely the intended expression is (G^2 + D^2) R^2 / (G^2 + D^2 + R^2) or similar, to be consistent with the structure of c_A = G^2 + (D^2 R^2)/(R^2 + D^2). The authors should verify and correct this expression.

comments.title: c_{p_O} in Theorem 4.1 depends on t but used as constant
comments.explanation: The definition of c_{p_O} in Theorem 4.1 involves a sum over τ up to t, making it time-dependent. However, in the bound (6), c_{p_O} appears inside c_1 and c_2 which are presented as constants (no t subscript). This is inconsistent: if c_{p_O} depends on t, then c_1 and c_2 should also depend on t, and the bound would not be a one-round progress bound in the usual sense. The authors should clarify whether c_{p_O} is meant to be a constant (perhaps using the maximum over t) or should be indexed by t.

comments.title: Missing justification for learning rate bound in Theorem 4.5
comments.quote: Assume $\{f_{t,i}(\bm{\omega})\}$ satisfy Assumption 1–4, the output of FedAvg has expected error smaller than $\epsilon$, for $\eta_{g}=1$ and $\eta_{l}\leq\frac{\sqrt{3+4(1+B^{2}+A^{2})}-\sqrt{4(1+B^{2}+A^{2})}}{6KL\sqrt{1+B^{2}+A^{2}}}$.
comments.explanation: The bound on $\eta_l$ is stated without derivation or reference to where it comes from. The expression involves $A$, $B$, $K$, $L$, but the paper does not explain how this bound is derived from Theorem 4.1 or from the assumptions. Since this bound is critical for the convergence rate results, the authors should either provide a derivation or cite the specific step in the proof where it appears. Add a brief justification or a reference to the appendix (e.g., Appendix B.4) after the bound.

comments.title: Missing statistical significance details for reported accuracies
comments.quote: We carefully tune the hyper-parameters in all algorithms (details refer to Appendix C.2.1), and report the optimal results (i.e. mean test accuracy across the past 5 best epochs) after repeated trials.
comments.explanation: The paper reports mean accuracies with standard deviations but does not specify the number of repeated trials. Without knowing the number of seeds, the reader cannot assess the reliability of the reported standard deviations or perform statistical significance tests. The authors should explicitly state the number of independent trials (e.g., 5 random seeds) and ideally report confidence intervals or p-values for the key comparisons (e.g., CFL-Core-Set vs. FedAvg on CIFAR10). Fix: Add a sentence such as 'All results are averaged over 5 independent trials with different random seeds.'

comments.title: Contradiction in dataset partitioning description
comments.quote: All datasets are partitioned to 210 subsets for 7 distinct clients: all clients are selected and trained for 500 communication rounds, and each client randomly samples one of the corresponding 30 subsets for the local training
comments.explanation: The text states that each client randomly samples one of the corresponding 30 subsets. However, if there are 7 clients and 210 subsets total, each client would have 30 subsets (210/7 = 30). The phrase 'one of the corresponding 30 subsets' is ambiguous: does each client have 30 subsets assigned to them, or does each client randomly sample from the entire pool of 210 subsets? The former interpretation is more natural but contradicts the phrase 'randomly samples one of the corresponding 30 subsets' because 'corresponding' suggests a fixed assignment, which would not be random sampling from a time-varying distribution. The authors should clarify whether the subsets are pre-assigned per client or drawn randomly each round from the full set. Rewrite to: 'each client randomly draws one subset from its own pool of 30 pre-assigned subsets' or 'each client randomly draws one subset from the full set of 210 subsets each round.'

comments.title: Table 3 reports improvement metrics inconsistently
comments.quote:
Metric | Improvement on Fashion-MNIST (%) | Improvement on CIFAR10 (%) | Improvement on CIFAR100 (%)  |
|  Absolute (Rr - Rf) | 0.27 | 0.35 | 0.72  |
|  Ratio ((Rr - Rf)/Rf) | 0.31 | 0.
comments.explanation: The improvement metrics in Table 3 are labeled as 'Absolute (Rr - Rf)' and 'Ratio ((Rr - Rf)/Rf)', but the values for the ratio are given as percentages (0.31%, 0.50%, 1.44%). However, the ratio should be a dimensionless number, not a percentage. For example, on Fashion-MNIST, (87.02 - 86.75)/86.75 = 0.00311, which is 0.311%, not 0.31%. The authors likely multiplied by 100 to express as a percentage but then labeled the column as '%'. This is inconsistent: if the column is in percent, the absolute improvement should also be in percent (0.27 percentage points), but the ratio should be 0.31% (not 0.31). The authors should either report the ratio as a decimal (0.0031) or clearly label the column as 'Ratio (%)' and compute correctly. Fix: Change the ratio row to '0.31%', '0.50%', '1.44%' or convert to decimals: '0.0031', '0.0050', '0.0144'.

comments.title: Information loss measurement is not actually shown
comments.quote: Figure 1 depicts that the performance of models is highly linked to the value of information loss: the lesser the information loss, the higher the performance.
comments.explanation: Figure 1 is described as plotting 'information loss' on the left y-axis, but the paper never defines how information loss is computed or measured for the core set method. Definition 3.1 defines information loss as the gradient difference between true and approximated objectives, yet the figure appears to plot something else (likely the core set size or a proxy). Without specifying the metric on the left axis and how it relates to Definition 3.1, the claim that performance is 'highly linked to the value of information loss' is unverifiable. The authors should either explicitly state how information loss is computed for this figure (e.g., as the norm of the gradient difference on a held-out set) or relabel the axis to reflect what is actually measured.

comments.title: Overlap experiments do not control for total data volume
comments.quote: We further relax the difficulty of federated continual learning, from challenging non-overlapping time-varying heterogeneous data (e.g. in Table 2 and Table 3) to a moderate time-varying case (i.e. the local data evolves with the overlapping, while the size of local datasets stay unchanged).
comments.explanation: The description states that the size of local datasets stays unchanged as overlap varies, but this creates a confound: if the total dataset is fixed and overlap increases, each client sees fewer unique data points over time (more repetition). The observed performance invariance across overlap levels (Table 5) could simply reflect that the effective number of distinct training examples per client decreases with overlap, canceling out any benefit from reduced non-iid-ness. A cleaner design would fix the number of unique examples per client and vary only the overlap proportion, or equivalently, increase the total dataset size proportionally with overlap. Without this control, the conclusion that 'the overlap parameter has no obvious connection with the final global test performance' is not supported.

comments.title: Stateless client scenario contradicts core set method's premise
comments.quote: However, due to the nature of stateless under the privacy concern, the advancements of core set method in CFL algorithm cannot be seen in this scenario.
comments.explanation: This statement is vague and appears to contradict the core set method's own design. A core set is a subset of past data retained locally; in a stateless scenario where each client appears only once, there is no past data to retain, so the core set method should be inapplicable, not merely 'not seen.' The paper should explain why core sets cannot be used (e.g., privacy constraints prevent storing data across rounds) rather than implying the method fails. If the core set method is inapplicable, it should be excluded from the comparison, not included with a non-result. The current phrasing is misleading.

comments.title: Table 4 missing standard deviations for CFL-Regularization + FedProx at α=0.2
comments.explanation: The entry for CFL-Regularization + FedProx at α=0.2 shows a standard deviation of ±0.01, which is implausibly small compared to all other entries (which range from ±0.07 to ±0.36). A standard deviation of 0.01 over repeated trials with neural network training suggests either a single trial was run (contradicting 'repeated trials') or a rounding/typographical error. The authors should verify this value and report the number of trials used for all entries. If only one trial was run, the ± notation is inappropriate.
